---
title: "DATA SCIENCE BOOTCAMP 2026"
subtitle: "PART 2: OPEN SCIENCE"
author:
  - name: Martin Ho
    email: martinkh.ho@mail.utoronto.ca
date: "2026-07-15"
date-format: long
bibliography: references.bib
csl: american-medical-association.csl
notes-after-punctuation: true
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: solarized
    css: slides.css
    chalkboard: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    navigation-mode: linear
    controls: true
    controls-auto: false
    controls-layout: edges
    link-external-newwindow: true
    footer: "Data Science Bootcamp 2026 В· Part 2: Open Science"
    logo: assets/images/git_logo.png
---


## What you will learn

By the end of this session, you should be able to:

- Explain how open science supports transparent and reproducible research
- Describe the role of FOSS, Git, GitHub, and a well-structured repository
- Write simple Markdown and understand how Quarto combines prose, code, and output
- Use `renv` to record an R project's package environment


## Declaration of AI-assisted technologies

All instructional material in this presentation was written by the author. OpenAI Codex (GPT-5.6-Terra, reasoning: high) helped format the slide deck. The author reviewed, edited, and verified the suggestions as needed and takes full responsibility for the final product.


## Why open science?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Research should be discoverable, inspectable, and reusable
- Sharing code and materials enables validation, replication, and reuse
- Clear workflows make study decisions easier to understand and review
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
![Potential threats to the scientific method [@munafo2017manifesto]](assets/images/nature-s415620160021-fig-001.png){fig-alt="Diagram of threats to the scientific method" width="100%"}
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Open science is not just about making outputs publicвЂ”it is about making research **usable and trustworthy**.
:::


## Open science concepts

::: {.compact}

| Principle | Focus |
|---|---|
| **FAIR** [@wilkinson2016fair] | Research outputs are findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable. |
| **Transparency** | Study decisions, definitions, and methods can be understood. |
| **Reproducibility** | The workflow can be run, checked, and updated. |

:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Make research easier to find and use

- Metadata and documentation
- Clear licensing
- Persistent repositories
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### Make research easier to verify

- Protocols and reporting checklists
- Data, code, and supporting materials
- Versioned computational environments
:::
:::


## Free and open source software (FOSS)

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Free software [@gnuproject2026what]

Focuses on the user's freedoms to run, study, modify, and distribute software.
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### Open source software [@opensourceinitiative2007open]

Focuses on the practical benefits of an open development model: collaboration, quality, security, and innovation.
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
FOSS combines these perspectives: source code is available under a license that permits use, study, modification, and sharing.
:::


## FOSS creates both opportunity and dependency

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="47%"}
![Critical dependencies [@xkcddependency]](assets/images/dependency.png){fig-alt="Cartoon illustrating software dependency chains" height="400px" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="47%"}
- FOSS is maintained by communities, organizations, and volunteers
- Widely used tools can become critical infrastructure
- A neglected or compromised dependency can affect many downstream projects
- Licenses determine how software can be reused and distributed
:::
:::


## Open science across the research lifecycle

<br>

![Open science principles throughout the research process [@centerforopensciencewhat]](assets/images/lifecycle-practices.png){fig-alt="Open science practices across the research lifecycle" width="90%" fig-align="center"}


## GitHub is for active work; repositories preserve snapshots

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### GitHub

- Hosts Git repositories for version control and collaboration
- Supports issues, pull requests, and code review
- Best suited to active project development
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### OSF and Zenodo

- Preserve long-term project snapshots
- Assign persistent DOIs to releases
- Support citation and discovery of research outputs
:::
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
![](assets/images/osf_logo.png){fig-alt="Open Science Framework logo" width="55%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
![](assets/images/zenodo_logo.png){fig-alt="Zenodo logo" width="55%"}
:::
:::


## Code editing: text files make change visible

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Text files

- Code: `.R`, `.py`
- Markup: `.md`, `.qmd`, `.html`
- Configuration: `.yaml`, `.json`
- Git can show line-by-line changes
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### Binary files

- Images, PDFs, and executables
- Require specific software to view
- Git can track changes, but cannot easily show their contents
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Plain-text source files are portable, reviewable, and well suited to version control.
:::


## VS Code and RStudio serve different priorities

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### VS Code

- Broad extension ecosystem
- Strong Git and GitHub integration
- Supports many languages and coding assistants
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### RStudio

- Polished interactive R workflow
- Console and Environment panes for exploration
- Familiar project-oriented interface
:::
:::

Both can edit scripts, run code, use terminals, and work with Git. Choose the environment that fits the task and your team.


## Git records a project's history

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
Git is a distributed version-control system. Each contributor has a local copy of the repository and its history.

1. **Stage** the changes for a snapshot
2. **Commit** the snapshot with a meaningful message
3. **Push** commits to a shared remote repository
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
```text
script.R
script_draft1.R
script_final.R
```

This becomes one file with a traceable history of commits.
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Git turns a collection of files into an auditable record of decisions and changes.
:::


## GitHub: remote, local, branches, and forks

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
![Remote and local repositories [@weberpals2025lets]](assets/images/remote_local.png){fig-alt="Diagram showing local and remote repositories" width="88%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
- A **remote** repository is hosted on GitHub; each collaborator has a **local** copy
- A **branch** isolates a proposed change from the default branch
- A **pull request** proposes merging a branch after review
- A **fork** is your own copy of a repository when you do not have write access
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
- The practical GitHub setup and click-by-click workflow are intentionally omitted from this lecture deck.
:::


## Coding assistants can support development

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- Explain unfamiliar code
- Summarize files and changes
- Translate code across languages
- Draft tests and documentation
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
- Suggest implementations and refactors
- Help review pull requests
- Identify opportunities for optimization
- Assist with README files and project documentation
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Treat generated suggestions as drafts: review them, test them, and remain responsible for the final work [@weberpals2025lets].
:::


## A good repository structure separates concerns

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
```text
.
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ analysis/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ docs/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ output/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ src/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ .gitignore
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ LICENSE
в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
- Keep code, data, documentation, and outputs clearly separated
- Use relative paths so the project works on another computer
- Keep generated or sensitive files out of Git
- Make the entry points and project purpose easy to find
:::
:::


## `.gitignore`, README, and LICENSE

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="32%"}
### `.gitignore`

Excludes generated or sensitive files from future commits. It does not untrack files already committed.
:::

::: {.column width="32%"}
### `README.md`

Explains the project's purpose, how to get started, how to get help, and who maintains it.
:::

::: {.column width="32%"}
### `LICENSE`

States the permissions others receive to use, modify, and redistribute the project.
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Making a repository public without a license does **not** grant others permission to reuse it.
:::


## Markup makes plain text publishable

Markdown uses simple symbols to describe structure and formatting [@xie2026markdown].

::: {.compact}

| What you want | Markdown |
|---|---|
| Heading | `# Main heading` |
| Bold / italics | `**bold**`, `*italics*` |
| List | `- Item` or `1. Item` |
| Link | `[Text](https://example.com)` |
| Image | `![Caption](image.png)` |
| Inline code | `` `code` `` |

:::

::: {.key-point}
Markdown is easy to edit, share, render, and track with Git.
:::


## Quarto combines narrative, code, and results

Literate programming interweaves human-readable documentation with source code and output [@knuth1984literate].

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### YAML metadata

```yaml
---
title: "My study"
format: html
toc: true
---
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### Executable code

```r
library(readr)
df <- read_csv("data.csv")
```
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
One Quarto document can render to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and other formats.
:::


## Quarto supports transparent reporting

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- Documents can include code, figures, tables, equations, and references
- Code can be shown, hidden, or folded depending on the audience
- R, Python, Julia, and other languages can be used in the same publishing system
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
$$
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_p X_p
$$

```text
project/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ analysis.qmd
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data/
в””в”Ђв”Ђ output/
```
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Quarto makes it easier to keep the explanation, analysis, and rendered output aligned.
:::


## `renv` records R package environments

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### When starting a project

```r
renv::init()
```

Creates a project library and `renv.lock`, which records package versions.
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
### When sharing a project

```r
renv::snapshot()
renv::restore()
```

Records the current environment and recreates it on another machine.
:::
:::

::: {.key-point}
Commit `renv.lock` and the activation files; usually ignore the project package library itself [@usheyintroduction].
:::


## Key takeaways

1. Open science makes research outputs more discoverable, inspectable, and reusable.
2. FOSS enables collaboration but introduces dependencies and licensing responsibilities.
3. Git and GitHub provide version history, review, and collaboration around text-based projects.
4. Markdown and Quarto turn plain text, code, and results into reproducible documents.
5. `renv` records package versions so an R project can be recreated more reliably.


## Further readings

- Open Source Guides [@github2026open]
- HARPER template [@wang2023harmonized]
- Reproducible analytic workflows [@weberpals2024fairification]
- R markdown (applying the principles to Quarto) [@xie2026markdown]


## References {#refs}

<script>
window.addEventListener("load", function () {
  function fitReferenceList() {
    const isPrintView = window.location.search.includes("print-pdf");
    const slide = isPrintView
      ? document.getElementById("refs")
      : Reveal.getCurrentSlide();
    if (!slide || slide.id !== "refs") return;

    const references = slide.querySelector(":scope > .references");
    if (!references) return;

    references.style.fontSize = "";
    const maximum = parseFloat(window.getComputedStyle(references).fontSize);
    const minimum = 10;
    const slideBox = slide.getBoundingClientRect();
    const referencesBox = references.getBoundingClientRect();
    const availableHeight = slideBox.bottom - referencesBox.top - 8;

    if (referencesBox.height <= availableHeight) return;

    let lower = minimum;
    let upper = maximum;
    for (let iteration = 0; iteration < 12; iteration += 1) {
      const size = (lower + upper) / 2;
      references.style.fontSize = `${size}px`;
      if (references.getBoundingClientRect().height <= availableHeight) {
        lower = size;
      } else {
        upper = size;
      }
    }
    references.style.fontSize = `${lower}px`;
  }

  fitReferenceList();
  Reveal.on("ready", fitReferenceList);
  Reveal.on("slidechanged", function (event) {
    if (event.currentSlide.id === "refs") {
      window.requestAnimationFrame(fitReferenceList);
    }
  });
  window.addEventListener("resize", function () {
    window.requestAnimationFrame(fitReferenceList);
  });
});
</script>
